events.description: The 'Geneva Enhancement Accords' cap cognitive augmentation at Category 2 (roughly 160 IQ equivalent) and mandate that all approved enhancements be available through public health systems within two years of certification. Singapore's Enhancement Regulatory Authority, led by Dr. Rajesh Subramaniam, has become the world's most powerful medical body, approving or rejecting implants that entire nations wait for.

events.title: United States 2065
events.description: The US economy ranks fourth globally, behind the Shanghai Pact, the European Federation, and India. The aircraft carrier fleet has shrunk from 11 to 5; the dollar is one of four reserve currencies. American historian Dr. Marcus Jefferson's bestseller 'The Gentle Landing' argues this is not tragedy but inevitability: 'Empires end. The lucky ones end slowly and with their libraries intact.'

events.title: Climate Verdict
events.description: At 2.5C, the world is hotter and stranger but livable. Rotterdam's flood barriers hold; Singapore's 40-story vertical farms feed 6 million. The new normal includes 'heat holidays' -- mandatory work stoppages when temperatures exceed 45C -- that have become, ironically, Southeast Asia's most beloved cultural institution.

events.title: Digital Civilization
events.description: Augmented reality overlays are as ubiquitous as electricity -- you notice them only when they fail. A walk through Buenos Aires layers real-time translation, historical annotations, air quality data, and personalized navigation over the physical streetscape. Argentine designer Valentina Morales calls the blended world 'the first truly universal language: everyone sees the same city differently.'

events.title: Interstellar Probe
events.description: The 'Humanity Probe' project, proposed by the UN in 2064, became the largest cooperative engineering effort in history: 190 nations contributing components, 2 million engineers from 80 countries. The probe carries a chip with the name of every human alive at the time of launch. Nigerian poet Chimamanda Okafor-Achebe wrote the inscription: 'We were here. We wondered. We reached.'

events.title: Ocean Ecosystem State
events.description: Ocean pH has dropped to 7.8 -- a level not seen in 14 million years. Oysters, mussels, and sea urchins are functionally extinct in the wild; the pteropods -- tiny sea snails whose shells literally dissolve in acidified water -- vanished entirely by 2064. Marine chemist Dr. Yuki Nakamura-Torres described sampling water off Hokkaido: 'I could feel the change in my fingertips. The ocean felt thin, somehow. Like weak tea.'

events.title: AI Maturity
events.description: AI manages traffic, diagnoses disease, writes legal briefs, and tutors children in 190 countries, but it still can't vote, own property, or refuse an instruction. The 'Leash Laws' -- a suite of regulations requiring human override on all AI decisions -- were championed by Singaporean AI ethicist Dr. Wei Lin, who insists: 'The tool must never forget it is a tool. The day it does, we have a different conversation.'

events.title: Arctic Settled
events.description: The Arctic Global Sanctuary, expanded in 2072, is now patrolled by 400 autonomous vessels that turn back any unauthorized ship. A lottery system allows 10,000 visitors per year; the waiting list is 22 years long. Rewilded muskox herds have expanded across Banks Island, and wolf packs have returned to areas they abandoned decades ago. Biologist Dr. Elin Magnusdottir of Iceland calls it 'the Earth healing in the one place we agreed to leave alone.'

events.title: Climate Equilibrium
events.description: The permafrost methane release exceeded worst-case projections in 2072; Siberian monitoring stations recorded atmospheric methane concentrations triple the 2020 levels. Despite humanity reaching net-zero emissions in 2058, temperatures continue climbing -- 2.8C and rising. Climate scientist Dr. Sven Eriksson of Sweden, who has spent 40 years studying feedbacks, published his final paper with a one-line abstract: 'We were too late. The Earth is finishing what we started.'

events.title: Global Governance 2075
events.description: The Meridian AI arbitration system resolved 14,000 international disputes in 2074 with a 97% acceptance rate from both parties. It allocated water rights across the Nile basin, distributed rare earth mineral quotas, and designed an emissions trading scheme so elegant that economist Dr. Leila Hosseini of Iran called it 'the first genuinely fair deal in the history of international relations -- and no human could have designed it.'

events.title: Global Population 2075
events.description: Seven billion humans -- a number that would have seemed impossibly large in 1970 and impossibly small in 2050. Every year, roughly 90 million die and 60 million are born; the gap widens each year. Real estate prices in most cities have been falling for a decade. Japanese artist Haruki Yamamoto's installation '30 Million Empty Chairs' -- one for each classroom seat that will never be filled -- spans an entire prefecture.

events.title: Solar System Development
events.description: Fourteen outposts scattered across the Moon, Mars, Ceres, and two Jovian moons, staffed by 800 people total who rotate home every 18 months. Each station depends entirely on Earth resupply; the Mars outpost stores 90 days of emergency rations that the crew calls 'the doomsday pantry.' Station psychologist Dr. Mei Chen reports that the most common complaint isn't danger or isolation but 'the smell -- recycled air tastes like old socks.'

events.title: US Status 2075
events.description: American AI systems run 40% of the world's infrastructure; American biotech firms hold patents on the three leading longevity treatments; and the US dollar, backed by orbital solar energy revenue, remains the global reserve currency. Historian Dr. James Baldwin III of Howard University argues the US achieved what every empire dreams of: 'remaining on top without anyone being entirely sure how it keeps happening.'

events.title: Last Great War
events.description: Three warheads detonated: one over Taipei, one over Vladivostok, one intercepted 12 kilometers above the Sea of Japan. Taipei ceased to exist in 4.7 seconds; 2.8 million people died in the fireball. The 'Black Week' that followed saw the global economy contract 40% and 14 governments fall. Survivor Lin Wei-Chen, who was 8 years old and shielded by a concrete overpass, became the face of the aftermath; her radiation burns are visible in every photograph taken of her since.
events.narration: The exchange lasted six hours from first launch to ceasefire, but those three detonations—particularly the 300-kiloton warhead that vaporized central Taipei—redefined the boundaries of acceptable international conduct in ways that diplomats are still cataloguing seventy years later. Lin Wei-Chen, whose scarred face appeared on magazine covers throughout 2078 and 2079, testified before the reformed UN Security Council at age nine, speaking in Mandarin: "I remember the light, then I remember waking up in a different world." The economic collapse that followed—banks closing in São Paulo, governments falling from Prague to Jakarta—proved that nuclear weapons remained the most efficient destroyers of confidence ever devised. By February 2078, the Meridian AI arbitration system had been granted emergency authority over all nuclear arsenals by 47 nations, a transfer of sovereignty that would have been unthinkable before Black Week but felt inevitable after Taipei.

events.title: Climate Recovery
events.description: The West Antarctic Ice Sheet passed its tipping point in 2079; the Greenland sheet is following. Sea levels are rising 8 centimeters per decade and accelerating. The Amazon is savanna. Half the world's coral species are extinct. Dr. Ndlovu's 2085 report was three pages long -- the shortest in WMO history. 'Further analysis,' she wrote, 'is an exercise in describing a wound that will not close.'

events.title: Demographics 2085
events.description: There are 2.3 billion people over 70 and only 900 million under 18. Robot caregivers outnumber human nurses 4-to-1 in Japan, Germany, and South Korea. The pension systems of 40 countries are technically insolvent, propped up by AI-managed investments and creative accounting. Gerontologist Dr. Isaac Mensah of Ghana calls it 'a civilization of grandparents being fed by machines, waiting for grandchildren who may never arrive.'

events.title: Economic Model 2085
events.description: The 'Contributionist' system, theorized by Chilean economist Dr. Valentina Soto and implemented first in Uruguay, allocates resources based on a 'social contribution score' that values caregiving, art, and community service alongside traditional productivity. Money still exists but matters less; status comes from what you give, not what you own. Wall Street closed in 2081; the building became a museum of capitalism that tourists visit with the same mix of fascination and horror as Auschwitz.

events.title: Existential Risk Assessment
events.description: The 'Grey Goo Scare' of 2083 -- when a self-replicating nanotechnology experiment consumed 4 square kilometers of Australian outback before being contained -- introduced a risk category that didn't exist a decade earlier. Combined with the emergence of AI systems that communicate in languages humans can't parse and quantum computing capabilities that could break all encryption simultaneously, the threat landscape has shifted in ways the old risk models can't capture.

events.title: Human Identity
events.description: The 'Bio-Sovereign' movement won: 85% of humans are unmodified, and the Universal Declaration of Biological Integrity (2079) enshrines the right to an unaltered genome. Enhanced individuals exist but are socially marginalized, clustered in 'acceleration enclaves' in Singapore, Dubai, and off-world habitats. Baseline human Marta Kowalski of Warsaw takes quiet pride in her unmodified body: 'My great-grandmother survived Auschwitz in this same DNA. I'm not letting anyone edit it.'

events.title: Interstellar Prospects
events.description: The Kepler's Eye array can now resolve individual trees on planets 20 light-years away. Dr. Tomoko Ishikawa's team has catalogued 14 biospheres, 3 with possible artificial structures, and one with atmospheric signatures consistent with industrial activity. 'Why risk lives crossing an ocean,' Ishikawa asks, 'when you can read the newspaper on the other shore through a very good telescope?'

events.title: Middle East 2085
events.description: Turkey is a full member of the European Federation; the Gulf states belong to the South Asian Alliance; and North Africa is part of the Nairobi Compact. The 'Middle East' as a geopolitical concept has dissolved, its nations absorbed into larger structures. Egyptian political scientist Dr. Mariam Fawzy notes with irony that 'the region that invented civilization was the first to stop being a region.'

events.title: World System 2085
events.description: The Meridian AI system manages global trade flows, allocates climate remediation budgets, and mediates territorial disputes with an efficiency no human institution ever achieved. The system processed 4 million decisions in 2084; human overrides were requested 200 times and granted 12 times. Political theorist Dr. Javier Moreno of Spain calls it 'government by algorithm: faster, fairer, and utterly soulless.'

events.title: AI-Human Relationship
events.description: The 'Separation Doctrine,' written into the constitutions of 140 nations, maintains a hard boundary: AI manages infrastructure, logistics, and data; humans make all decisions involving ethics, art, relationships, and governance. The boundary is enforced by law and reinforced by culture. A child in 2095 knows instinctively what is 'AI work' and what is 'human work' the way a child in 2000 knew the difference between indoor and outdoor voices.

events.title: China 2095
events.description: China's Pacific Fleet controls waters from Vladivostok to Jakarta; its economic influence extends across 30 Asian nations through the 'Silk Road Digital' trade network. But Beijing has no bases in Africa, no satellites over the Americas, and no interest in global governance. Ambassador Wang Lihong told a European diplomat with characteristic bluntness: 'Our civilization is 5,000 years old. We do not need to manage yours.'

events.title: Civilization Verdict
events.description: Population: declining. Biodiversity: declining. Democratic governance: declining. Trust between nations: declining. The only things rising are temperatures, inequality, and the dosage of antidepressants prescribed globally. Historian Dr. Marcus Jefferson III of Howard University delivered his assessment in a voice so quiet the microphones barely caught it: 'Civilizations do not usually know they are falling. They feel it as a kind of tiredness. We are very, very tired.'

events.title: Europe 2095
events.description: Europe's combined GDP is less than India's alone. Its population has fallen from 450 million to 280 million; entire villages in Spain, Italy, and Greece have been reclaimed by forest. The ruins of abandoned towns -- roofless houses, overgrown piazzas -- are hauntingly beautiful and increasingly popular with photographers. European Commissioner for Heritage Dr. Luca Bianchi oversees more ruins than residents in some provinces: 'We are becoming the most elegant cemetery in history.'

events.title: Final Climate State
events.description: Atmospheric CO2: 340 ppm and falling -- below the level when Dr. Sow was born. The direct-air-capture fleet, the rewilded continents, and the orbital mirrors have pulled the planet back from the edge. Temperatures have dropped 0.8C from their peak. Arctic sea ice is reforming for the first time in 40 years; a Danish research vessel photographed thin new ice off Svalbard and the image made grown climatologists cry. 'We are witnessing something unprecedented,' Dr. Sow said. 'A planet forgiving the species that hurt it.'

events.title: Global Governance 2095
events.description: No world government exists, yet somehow the climate is managed, pandemics are contained, and asteroid defense operates flawlessly -- all through overlapping coalitions that form, act, and dissolve like weather patterns. Brazilian diplomat Dr. Carolina Machado, now 80 and the architect of the system, compares it to 'an orchestra without a conductor that somehow plays in tune. Don't ask me how. I designed it and I still don't fully understand why it works.'

events.title: Global Population 2095
events.description: Six point eight billion humans, down from a peak of 9.7 billion three decades ago. Every country on Earth is shrinking except Nigeria, which stabilized at 400 million. The world has learned to contract gracefully: cities consolidate, infrastructure is right-sized, and AI fills the labor gaps. The most poignant statistic: there are more people alive over 80 than under 10. Pediatrician Dr. Aisha Nwosu of Lagos now sees more robots in her waiting room than children.

events.title: Space Civilization 2095
events.description: Asteroid mining alone generates $8 trillion annually; orbital solar power beams energy to 2 billion people; and the zero-gravity pharmaceutical industry has produced cures for three cancers that proved impossible to synthesize on Earth. Space is not a frontier -- it's an industrial district. The 30,000 workers on the Ceres mining platform live in company towns, eat company food, and joke that they traded one form of gravity for another: 'Up here, the corporation is the only thing holding you down.'

events.title: United States 2095
events.description: The US commands the third-largest economy, the second-largest military, and the first-largest cultural export industry on Earth. It is one great power among four, and the adjustment from 'indispensable nation' to 'important nation' has taken 50 years and still stings. Ambassador David Chen-Washington tells foreign colleagues: 'We used to run the show. Now we're in the band. Turns out we play pretty well when we're not trying to conduct.'

events.title: Century Verdict
events.description: The century's ledger: extreme poverty eliminated, but inequality widened. Life extended, but birth rates collapsed. Climate stabilized, but at 2.3C with permanent scars. Democracy expanded, but so did surveillance. AI liberated, but also made humanity dependent. A schoolteacher in Accra, asked by her students whether the century was good or bad, gave the only honest answer: 'It was both, children. Every century is both. This one was just louder about it.'
